sentences.text: Excuse me.
After-- when I found my brother, we're hugging and kisses and everything.
So the farmer came up the next day-- we stayed in the barn overnight.
And the next day-- at night they took us in to another location, to a stable of cows.
Cows were down below, and we were on top.
They put us on top.
Only lying accommodation.
No stand up.
It's a small attic there, a small place to-- to accommodate.
It was very tight.

So we were lying there and didn't take long-- no, but the farmer took us down every morning for breakfast. 5 o'clock in the morning, we had a hot breakfast, potatoes and borscht and whatever he's having.
And-- and he put us right back because he was afraid of the neighbors.
Anybody would find out he could get shot, burned, the whole house and everything.
He took a big chance anyway, but he kept us.
We were there maybe I would say a month on the-- on the winter.
It was warm.
There was straws around us.
But it-- it didn't last long.
All of a sudden, my brother develops a temperature on the-- he's burning like a fire and all.
He must've been 102.
And every day gets worse, and I knew already that's-- that's the-- there was no doctors.
There's nobody, nothing else.
And-- and they gave us during the day food from the-- from the stable.
There's a hole in the-- on the top.
He gave us water, to-- lunch, whatever.
We ate, but he was sick.
And it was maybe a week, took a week, maybe 10 days.
The last few days, he was hysterical.
Everything came out of his mouth white, the saliva and all.
I was-- it was very frightening.
He was burning like a match, and he start to talk, you know, silly things.
And at the end he said, where you gonna bury me?
You know, I'm gonna die.
He's dying, yep.
I said, don't worry.
We'll see.
This is not a problem.
And, well, I was told that the typhoid, if it had breaks to certain 12 day, if you break the-- the fever, the temperature, you'll survive.
And all of a sudden, one morning we got up to go down for bathing, go down.
I told him you're not going down for breakfast.
And his color changed, and the temperature was down.
And I was very happy to see that.
So then I said this might turn.
We're not gonna survive here.
It's '42 and I know the war.
And I didn't hear too much about the war.
I knew that it went to Russia.
You know, they're fighting in Russia.
But we can't survive here for-- for a year or two or three.
Who knows how long.
I have to make a move.
So I sneaked out the middle of the night.
The farmer didn't know even.
I had a way how to get out.
And I-- there was a-- a potato hold-- no.
Cave that they kept potatoes, and I went into this.
I didn't want nobody-- I didn't want to be spotted by dogs or anybody so I waited.
It was middle of the night.
I waited for when it gets some daylight, you know?
A little daylight in the morning.
Then I started out to go and look for some connection maybe I'll find.
It was scary.
You know.
The dogs were barking.
Anyway, so I started to walk.
Where do I go?
I walk-- I ended up in my hometown, which was maybe three miles away from the village, four miles.
So where do I go?
I'm gonna go to [INAUDIBLE].
We knew already this Wenzel is gonna be our savior, whatever-- whatever I need.
So I walked in.
And they gave me an address.
He heard a rumor that this Malzman is hiding somewhere, and I should have to contact him to find him.
So when I-- we had shoes at this farmer too, and I knew I'm gonna need some shoes.
I took a pair of shoes from-- I knew where the shoes were lying there.
I put it under my coat and then I came into those-- those Polish people, which they knew-- and I gave them a pair of shoes.
Thought money for money.
Tell me.
She told me right away where it is.
So I went back to this place.
He was-- that place where I made that camp, [?
Ellen ?].
You remember?
In [INAUDIBLE].
And I walk in.
It was in broad daylight.
He walks around in pyjamas like-- like nothing happened.
And I saw him through the window and I walked in.
And I took a chance because I looked like-- nobody would recognize me.
It was a strange city.
They didn't know me.
And I don't look like Jewish, you know?
That's-- that's the whole purpose.
Nobody would know I was Jewish, even the German.
Nobody would know.
So I walked right in, and he started to-- where do you come from?
He didn't know what happened to me.
He was older than me, you know?
And I said, OK.
He-- he got in touch with a boy, Polish boy with a bicycle, and he gave him orders, take him out to the hiding place immediately.
And I left my brother there, you know, and I didn't know.
Then I said you got to go immediately.
And that was-- it took us maybe, this boy on the bike, on the bike two of us, and he took us to this hiding place.
It was five miles away from-- from the place around the camp where we worked in the beginning, you know, not far from there.
He took us in a forest which only you could get in on your stomach.
You can't walk.
A tight, very tight forest.
There he took me into the-- I could-- I wouldn't know.
I ask him, where you taking me?
I ask Malzman.
You'll see.
A surprise.
Anyway, and I got into this forest, and I got into the-- into the hiding place.
There was a little hole, an opening maybe like four squares of this, size of this, and covered up.
Nobody ever knew there's a hole there.
They dug a big hole the size of this room.
You know.
And they made with pillows on top.
You know.
They covered in branches and they put some tar paper, shouldn't-- water shouldn't get in.
A nice hiding place, but it was very low in the ground.
You know, it was warm, and it was winter.
You know.
I come into this place.
The first comes out is a lady, a woman with-- she had a braid.
She was dark, same hair like you.
She was a sister-in-law of this Malzman, his brother's wife.
And she walks out.
I never seen a sight like this.
She looked like a-- like a gypsy.
You know.
Dirty.
Unbelievable what I saw.
And I said, is this it?
And then they start coming out, one after another.
She had a little boy, three years old, was in that hiding.
And they start coming out.
And the boy, the Polish boy, went back with the bike and he left me there.
That's it.
You're with the-- with the rest of the gang.
There were nine of them, and she was the one woman and that boy.
And we stayed there in that hole.
But there was a problem with food.
So the boy-- the boy used to come every day bringing a loaf of bread, you know.
This-- this Malzman organized.
And then he came afterwards.
He came to the hole too.
He wasn't safe anymore.
You know, it was dangerous.
He came to us.
But-- but they needed help, more-- more food and this.
So I wrote a note with the-- with the boy, the Polish-- he was the messenger every day.
I wrote a note to-- to Wenzel.
He should send every-- I gave him a note-- sell whatever you have and try and buy food.
But food consist of one bread, which not butter, but it was like-- we call it schmaltz.
Whatever you call it in--
Fat.
Fat, you know.
He gave a little jar of fat and a bread, every day he send us with this messenger to the hole.
And that's what we had to live on.
But a bread was two kilo.
That's a big bread.
We cut up portions and gave everybody.
This is it.
Eat it now, later, or tomorrow.
It's your problem.
Tomorrow we'll get another one if the boy will come.
But it was a problem-- water.
We had no water.
So every day, every night after midnight-- he couldn't go during the day because it was dangerous.
There was a river maybe three miles away from the hiding.
The main river-- well, we were on the same river.
And we had to go, we had a pail.
We had a pail.
We had to go every-- every night two boys different, took shifts, had to go bring a pail of water.
We need water.
But by the time you came back-- goes at midnight, but they couldn't find the place, so we had a signal like whistles, you know, like-- like birds.
So we whistled, and they whistled back, and we knew already how to get in the two boys.
I went-- I had a few turns.
I went too.
It was very scary.
By time we came back with a pail of water-- wasn't a pail of water.
Was a half.
Well, you spill on the way.
You know, two or three miles they're walking a pail of water.
But how you get in the pail of water in the forest?
So we sneak through the pail.
I remember.
I went a few times.
We pushed through the pail.
And while pushing the pail, all the needles from the-- you know from this-- from the-- Pine?
From the spruce, from the trees went into the-- half a pail was water and the half a pail was-- anyway, that has to-- we took out all the things.
So when we got there with the water, we-- we ration the water, everybody should get so much.
So what happened, one day-- the day-- later-- this is like, well, maybe three, four days later.
I-- I get temperature in the hole.
So everybody's yelling, the water we have to save for [?
George. ?] He is sick.
Anyway, just to wipe-- just to wipe-- just to wet my lips, I'll never forget, and-- and that was it.
It was-- took-- took 12 days, and I broke the fever.
And then another boy took sick next to me, so we didn't want to keep him-- they kept me there.
They should've isolate me.
Well, then, when the other one got sick, they were afraid there's going to be an-- we're all going to die of the fever.
So dug a hole that was separate from us and they put him over there.
But he died anyway.
He died.
After when I left, I found out.
So I broke the fever and then I didn't-- I didn't know what to think.
And I said my brother's there.
What I'm going to do with him?
I have to bring him here.
So I wrote a note with the messenger-- to the farmer because he wouldn't let him in, let-- let-- his name was [?
Martin. ?] You know.
Let-- let him out and send eggs, whatever you can because after the-- after my sickness I was dying.
I had-- I could eat anything-- butter, this and that and bread.
He brought a whole bunch of-- a whole-- so they were yelling, this only goes for the sick.
Nobody's allowed.
Anyway, I was very happy he came.
That was November, end of November, and then December, and we're still there day after day.
We went out during the day-- during the day-- at night.
During the day we were in the hole.
We were afraid.
I mean, nobody was there.
Nobody would know.
And we went out middle of-- at night, we went out for fresh air, you know, and just walk around.
And night wasn't dangerous, not around-- around the hole.
But one day, the master-- the forest master, you called it.
He is in charge of the forest.
He spotted that somebody's in the forest.
He's in charge here.
Anyway, he found us, but, you know, he was Polish.
He said, I don't want to make no trouble, but get out of my area.
And that's it.
We had to move the whole thing to a different area, dug a new hole.
And then we needed tar paper so who's gonna bring tar paper because the rain-- sometimes-- it wasn't rain.
Already it was snow.
And we had to cover up.
So they sent me to the city, because I look like-- like a Pollack, you know.
So to bring a roll of tar.
You know-- can you imagine?
I'll never forget that.
I walked five miles with the roll.
Nobody knew where I'm going.
So we-- we-- and they knew-- and they knew our quarters, that was our new quarters.
[INAUDIBLE]-- day after day and Wenzel was sending, was sending food every day.
Same thing, same bread, same thing.
I mean, we survived.
And one day, this Malzman comes to me and whispers in my ears, you know, they wanted to-- he said, [?
George, ?] you know, we're not gonna-- we're not gonna live.
We're all gonna die here.
You got money.
I know you have money.
He knew that I have money.
I'll go into the city hall, I'm not afraid.
I have some connection.
I'm gonna make papers, forged-- no.
We-- it wasn't forged.
It was original papers from the city hall identify as Polish names.
My name was [?
Gora. ?] Bronislaw [?
Bruno ?] [?
Gora. ?] My brother's name was [?
Andre ?] [INAUDIBLE].
You know.
And his was [INAUDIBLE], this-- this Malzman.
The three of us.
So I gave him enough money.
They wanted, I think, $100 for a passport, you know, under the table.
But the names were the original names, because those boys, they send them to Germany for-- for work.
You know, they-- they usually catch transport.
They send them, Polacks, to work for Germany, the German.
So that was original papers.
If they ever-- we ever get caught in Germany, they're gonna check back.
Sure, they were registered here.
You know.
It was-- it was done wonderful.
So we got-- one day we got the papers.
They says it'll take a week or two until we get them out.
And we got the papers, and we take off in middle of the night.
We didn't say goodbye to nobody.
And-- no.
It's not the story.
And then-- no.
We got three Gentile boys.
He organized three Gentile-- we had-- what we gonna do with the papers?
We have to go to an office to go volunteer to work to Germany as Polack.
But we can't go.
They used to give examination.
The minute they undress me, I'm finished-- we are finished.
We got three boys to go for us with the papers.
And they get-- they went through.
They got their permits stamped, so-- so they went with us.
We went six of us-- the Gentile, the three of them, they got paid.
And the-- the three of us.
Middle of the night, we sneaked out of the hole.
We went on the train, and we-- we traveled-- we went on the train.
There were all kind of SS checking, checking, but we had papers so no problem.
And the Gentile boys went with us.
I'll tell you why.
There's a reason.
You know.
Well, with us, if they-- we had our papers already, and they had their own papers, so there was no problem.
They checked it and it was OK.
We went through especially the middle of the night.
It was at night.
We arrived to the city of [INAUDIBLE].
There was a concentration camp-- not concentration like us, but the concentration to keep all the workers-- Russian, Ukraine, Polish, you know?
They kept them concentrated to ship them off to Germany.
When we got there, so this Malzman said, uh oh, I have a problem.
I saw a policeman which he knows me.
And there was no Jews.
I mean, with-- there was no Jews.
Finished.
So I've got to get out of here as fast as possible.
The minute there'll be an opening, I'll go.
Course, they took to a factory, they took to factory for work.
Myself, I didn't want to go to a factory.
I said, I don't want to go-- I want to go on the farm like my brother who was sick after the typhoid.
No, no.
We went to the examination afterwards.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
OK, so-- so what happened.
So we were-- this Malzman got a job right away, went to a-- he said, I don't care where I get as long getting it out of here because here I get caught right away.
As a matter of fact, his-- this woman was with us.
He took her afterwards.
I'm going to forget about it.
He maybe six month-- we were in Germany when he-- he organized for her.
She was still in the hole there, and she went to [INAUDIBLE], she got shot in [INAUDIBLE].
After the war we found out.
So what happened, so one-- he left.
We remained in the camp there maybe three weeks.
And then they took the males of the-- of the group, you know whatever-- Ukrainian, Polish, and this.
They put us in the train, and they took us into Germany because the males are supposed to go to farms because people who go to a factory-- we were after the typhoid.
You know, they checked the arm.
They didn't work.
They don't-- we were very weak.
You know.
It was in the end of '42.
So we got into Germany and-- and the two farmers came, two women.
They came into this-- they kept us in also a little camp.
They waited for-- for farmers to come to select us.
The two women selected us, myself and [?
Martin, ?] my brother.
Well, we like those two boys.
We'll take them.
They were two friends from the same village.
And I didn't like the idea of-- they got us into that village, and they gave my brother a farm and one woman took him, and the other one took me.
I work on her farm.
So I didn't like the idea because we shouldn't both be in the same village in case-- they didn't know we were brothers.
You know.
They were different names, but in case one gets caught-- so why should we both go?
So I worked a scheme to get out of it.
You know.
I stay there for a month, and I told her, my boss, you know, I'm afraid of horses, blah blah blah.
She took me back to the-- to the office, the city.
It wasn't far away from the city.
By the way, the place where we ended first was the name of the city was Bautzen.
Bautzen was a city between Dresden and Leipzig.
And-- and this-- this place we worked the first was [INAUDIBLE].
It was a little village.
Farmers, you know?
So-- so the woman took me back to the-- to the office from the-- for employment.
I said, who do you give me?
He's afraid of horses.
All right, they gave me another place.
So I'm out of this.
You know.
It's always working, Yiddish [INAUDIBLE].
So what happens, they gave me another place.
So was no good either.
What was that?
Was no good.
They-- they pushed me.
They would ship me off 40 miles away from my brother so that wouldn't work either.
So when I gonna see him?
Who knows?
We're not allowed to travel just like that.
We were Polish.
You know.
So what happens.
So I had a-- I don't know where it is.
I don't know.
One of my fingers.
So the farmer gave me to chop wood.
I chopped off a finger.
Said, I don't need people like this.
They took me back to the-- to the office, and they gave me another job.
And they didn't know what to do with me already.
They put me-- it wasn't far from my brother already, back.
It was maybe eight miles, so I used to see him.
He used to come to me.
And my kid brother, he was so clever.
And he came to visit me, to the other second-- to the third place already.
He came to visit me.
So there was a curfew.
You know, he went back at night, late at night, 11 o'clock.
And he spoke German perfectly.
He was young.
You know, he-- he was mixed up with the German and it was very wonderful.
And they-- they stopped him on the highway.
He was on a bicycle.
He came to visit me.
They stopped him on the highway to ask him [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
And he gets off the bike, he says Heil Hitler, you know.
And he speaks to them for a-- no, he's German so they let him go.
He was just like German.
Anyway, so what happened.
So I stayed in this place and this farmer.
It wasn't too bad.
I was happy.
And what happened, how did I get out of there?
You know, I don't remember.
Yeah, I got out of there.
So in that place, I worked with a-- with a [?
Tatar ?], you know, from [INAUDIBLE].
He came from [INAUDIBLE], a Russian.
He worked on the same farm.
On the same farm was a boy from Belgium.
I'll show you the picture out there where it is.
Belgium.
We worked together on the same farm, but those-- this Belgian was a war prisoner but he worked on the farm, and he went to sleep in the [INAUDIBLE], it's called.
Like a-- where they kept the war prisoners.
You know.
But he came to work on the farm.
And this wasn't so bad.
You know, they treated me very good.
I remember the-- the boss' wife, she always gave me an extra portion of food.
It was good.
You know.
Food wasn't missing.
Breakfast was served 6 o'clock in the morning.
You know.
And this farmer's wife had a son on the front, which he got killed, only son.
She took a liking to me, you know?
And she had-- she had bought a horse for her son, like a thoroughbred, you know.
Beautiful horse.
Said from now on, this is-- this is gonna be your horse.
You look after-- gives me memories for my son.
Anyway, this is a short story.
So we were there in this village, was a lot of Russian, and there was only one Polack, which I was very happy.
I don't want to get mixed up with the Polack.
It could've ruined everything.
They were very dangerous.
With the Russian you could get along.
So I stay there, and I don't know how-- how I got out of this farmer.
He took me back.
I don't remember the reason why he took me back.
But again, I'm back to the-- and that was 1944 already. 1944, took me back to the-- to the work-- to the unemployment office, you know, in Bautzen again.
They knew me already.
I was dressed nicely, and I was [? lookout ?] presenter.
Anyway, a guy comes in and said, that's the guy I want.
I'm going to take him.
Was like dealing-- it was slavery, you know?
That's the guy I would like to have.
Now, this man happened to be a baker.
What was his name?
But I'm through with the-- with the hiding.
I'm already through already.
The hiding was most stories but I'm going-- I have to-- you have to continue because I'm gonna miss a lot.
So his name was Schultz.
He selected me.
This, he looks like a businessman, and I need one like this.
So my job was to take supply.
He-- he had a bakery and he wasn't drafted to the army because he had to bake bread for the community or for the stores.
So your job is going to be to take 1,200 bread every day for distribute it to stores.
That's going to be your job every day. 11 o'clock in the morning, you go with the bread and bring me back the [INAUDIBLE]-- everything was Russian.
You had to bring back the coupons.
You know.
And it worked like a business-- a business deal.
Anyway, and they took away his car.
There was a motor attached to the wagon, so they took it away to the army.
So they gave us-- he had horses, he had a farm too.
So I had to drive the horses every day to the city with the-- with the bread.
And it was good and he was very good to me.
And it's-- before I left, he gave me a cigar.
Was very nice.
He was a young man, but-- but she-- his wife was a [? shlak. ?] You know, she didn't treat me too good.
And--
A what?
Oh, the girls?
Yeah, I'm coming to it, you know.
That was in '44.
And I was very happy.
One day-- one day the Russian weren't far from us.
You know, they already advance-- they came-- they advanced the front, and they pushed them back, the German.
It wasn't far.
So one day I went-- when I went to city, they bombed it.
It's a break?
Two minutes to go.
OK, oh two minutes.
Two more.
So what happened, so I went to the city, so they bombed and I had a splinter.
Got a splinter and a lot of people got injured.
They got them to the hospital, and I got in too.
The bandaged my hair and when I came back from-- from the city, I went to eat supper.
So I was sitting at the table so I didn't want to-- the blood was coming out, a little discharge, so I put a cap on.
My boss tells me, he says, Bruno, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You look like a Jew with a cap.
I said, but you only know what's underneath that cap.
So I made a-- I smiled and that-- I said, don't worry.
I can't sit like this any-- Anyway, that was the end.
So what happened?
So you stop now?
We'll stop now.
OK.
